era_context: Identical to -2525: late Old Kingdom Egypt (6th Dynasty), Akkad Empire under Naram-Sin at its height, mature Harappan civilization in the Indus Valley, and the semi-legendary Xia Dynasty period in China. This is a stable moment in three major civilizations before the environmental and political crises of the 22nd century BCE.

events.title: Pepi II's reign continues (traditional chronology)
events.description: Pepi II remains on the throne of Egypt in this year, according to traditional king-list chronology. His reign is characterized by declining central authority, increasing power of provincial governors (nomarchs), and economic strain. Inscriptions from his reign record expeditions to Nubia and the Red Sea, but the overall trend is toward fragmentation. No specific events are recorded for this particular year.

sources.name: Turin Papyrus and Abydos King List
Biographical inscriptions of officials from Pepi II's reign

events.certainty_note: Same as -2525-001. Pepi II's reign is traditionally dated to c. 2278–2184 BCE, placing this year within it, but absolute dating is uncertain by ±50–100 years.

events.title: Akkad Empire consolidation under Naram-Sin
events.description: Naram-Sin continues to consolidate Akkadian control over Mesopotamia and the Levant. His reign represents the zenith of Akkadian imperial power. Administrative reforms and military campaigns maintain the empire's territorial extent. The empire's economy is supported by long-distance trade and tribute from conquered territories. No specific events are recorded for this year, but the broader context is one of imperial stability before the empire's rapid collapse around 2200 BCE.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative tablets

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is dated c. 2254–2218 BCE. This year falls within that range. Specific year-by-year events are not preserved.

events.title: Harappan trade networks at peak activity
events.region: Indus Valley and beyond, South Asia
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization maintains extensive trade networks with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, Central Asia, and possibly Egypt. Seals and weights found in Mesopotamian sites (Ur, Lagash) and Persian Gulf trading posts (Dilmun) attest to this commerce. The standardized Harappan weights and measures facilitate long-distance exchange. No specific events are recorded for this year, but the material evidence of trade is abundant.

sources.name: Harappan seals and weights found in Mesopotamian contexts
Excavations at Dilmun (Bahrain) and Gulf trading posts

events.certainty_note: The existence of Harappan trade networks is confirmed by archaeological evidence of seals and artifacts in foreign contexts. The mature Harappan phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE) is well-dated.

disconfirming_evidence: No new disconfirming evidence specific to this year. The same chronological uncertainties apply as in -2525.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially undocumented in terms of specific events. History at this granular level (single year) is not preserved for any of the three major civilizations active in 2526 BCE. The 'history' presented here is inference from broader chronological frameworks and material culture. The absence of year-specific records reflects the limitations of ancient historiography.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's reign continues from the previous year.
Naram-Sin's reign continues from the previous year.
